title: Deed Claim (Specialty Contract)

statuteRef.label: Time limit for actions on a specialty

questions.label: Date of breach
questions.helpText: The date the breach of the deed or specialty contract occurred. For a deed, this is typically when the obligation under the deed was broken.

questions.label: Was the claimant under a disability at the date of accrual?
questions.helpText: Under the Limitation Act 1980, a person is under a disability if they are a minor (under 18) or lack capacity within the meaning of the Mental Capacity Act 2005.

questions.label: Date disability ceased
questions.helpText: The date the claimant turned 18, or the date capacity was regained.

questions.label: Was there fraud, deliberate concealment, or mistake?
questions.helpText: Under s.32 Limitation Act 1980, if the action is based on fraud, or the defendant deliberately concealed relevant facts, or the action is for relief from the consequences of a mistake, the limitation period does not begin until the claimant discovered or could with reasonable diligence have discovered the fraud, concealment, or mistake.

questions.label: Date of discovery
questions.helpText: The date the claimant discovered, or could with reasonable diligence have discovered, the fraud, concealment, or mistake.

questions.label: Has the defendant made a written, signed acknowledgment of the claim?
questions.helpText: Under s.29 Limitation Act 1980, a written acknowledgment signed by the person liable (or their agent) causes the limitation period to run afresh from the date of acknowledgment.

questions.label: Date of acknowledgment
questions.helpText: The date of the written, signed acknowledgment.

questions.label: Has the defendant made a part payment on the claim?
questions.helpText: Under s.29 Limitation Act 1980, a part payment by the person liable (or their agent) causes the limitation period to run afresh from the date of payment.

questions.label: Date of part payment
questions.helpText: The date the part payment was made.

notes: A deed must be executed as a deed (signed, witnessed, and delivered) to attract the 12-year period. If the document is not validly executed as a deed, the 6-year period for simple contracts under s.5 applies.
Equitable claims arising from a deed may be subject to the equitable doctrine of laches rather than the statutory limitation period.
